behaviors.description: Conversation history is trimmed to most recent 10 messages when exceeding context window
behaviors.justification: This is pure deterministic function logic with no external dependencies or I/O. A mock history array can verify the trimming boundary correctly. Real failures (off-by-one, wrong slice indices) are caught by simple input/output assertions.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: Create a history with 15+ messages, call the trim_history function, verify exactly the last 10 messages are selected and passed to the LLM prompt builder. Test boundary conditions: 10 messages (no trim), 11 messages (trim 1), 1 message (edge case).

behaviors.description: LLM-generated response is coherent, topically addresses the customer's question, and is not generic or off-topic
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends entirely on what the real LLM produces. A mock LLM can be instructed to return any response, masking real failures where the LLM misunderstands the question or produces generic boilerplate. Only repeated calls to a real LLM will reveal if it consistently addresses the question or drifts off-topic.
behaviors.test_description: Agentic test: Call the chatbot with real LLM API for 10+ distinct customer questions (ranging from simple to complex). Verify each response addresses the specific question asked, not a template response. Use heuristics (question keywords appear in response, response length appropriate, no obvious misunderstandings) or a secondary LLM evaluator to detect off-topic or generic replies.

behaviors.description: Tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration
behaviors.justification: Must test with a real tone checker (not mocked) to catch its classification errors—e.g., false negatives (formal response rated as casual) or false positives (casual response rated as formal). A mock tone checker that always returns 'too formal' hides the actual classifier's behavior. Integration with the real tone checker (whether rule-based, heuristic, or classifier) is minimum to catch its failures. Workflow is preferred if also verifying the regenerated response is actually more casual.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Generate a deliberately formal response (e.g., 'I acknowledge receipt of your inquiry...'), pass it through the real tone checker with casual-tier customer metadata. Verify the checker flags it as 'too formal' and triggers regeneration. Test negative case: generate a casual response and verify no false-positive regeneration. Optionally call real LLM for regeneration and verify the second response is materially more casual.

behaviors.description: Full conversation exchange (user message, generated response, regenerations if any) is persisted to database with correct, consistent timestamps
behaviors.justification: Requires a real database to catch failures that mocks hide: schema mismatches, null timestamp fields, incorrect datetime formats, transaction rollbacks, or missing fields. A mocked in-memory database will always accept writes and never reveal persistence bugs. Real DB integration catches actual storage behavior, index constraints, and timestamp generation/storage correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Call the chatbot end-to-end with real database. Retrieve the logged exchange from the DB. Verify all fields present: user_message, initial_response, any regenerated_response, timestamps for each message and response. Validate timestamps: monotonically increasing, in RFC3339 format, within ±5 seconds of test execution time. Test with regeneration: verify both initial and regenerated responses are in the log with distinct timestamps.

behaviors.description: When tone check triggers regeneration, the final response replaces the draft; customer never sees the rejected draft in API response or conversation history
behaviors.justification: Must verify the contract at two levels: (1) API response to customer shows only the final response, not the rejected draft, and (2) database conversation history shows only the final response. A unit test of response object construction could verify (1) in isolation, but (2) requires integration with real database. A mock DB hides whether the draft is being incorrectly written or left in history.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Trigger the full chatbot flow including tone check regeneration. Capture the API response sent to the customer and verify it contains only the final response, no mention of the draft or tone failure. Query the database conversation history for the exchange and verify only the final response is logged (draft is not stored, or stored but marked internal-only). Test edge case: verify the customer's UI doesn't receive a 'regenerating...' state that leaks the draft.
